name: The barren fig tree
notes: A twofold synoptic retelling of the cursing of the fig tree during Passion week, given by Matthew and Mark. The shared core runs detail for detail: the Lord hungering in the morning, seeing a leafy fig tree by the way, finding nothing but leaves, speaking the curse that no fruit grow on it for ever, and then the lesson on faith that moves mountains and on believing prayer. Matthew narrates the withering at once and the disciples marvelling, while Mark spreads the account across two days, with Peter calling the tree to remembrance and adding the word on forgiving when ye stand praying. This complementary vantage of timing is left as a matter for further study. Each evangelist keeps his own unhurried frame around the same words of the Lord.

links.label: in the morning ... he hungered ... he was hungry
saw a fig tree in the way ... a fig tree afar off having leaves
found nothing thereon but leaves only ... he found nothing but leaves
Let no fruit grow on thee henceforward for ever ... No man eat fruit of thee hereafter for ever
the fig tree withered away ... the fig tree dried up from the roots
the disciples saw it they marvelled ... Peter calling to remembrance ... is withered away
If ye have faith and doubt not ... Have faith in God
say unto this mountain Be thou removed and be thou cast into the sea
ask in prayer believing ye shall receive ... believe that ye receive them and ye shall have them
